context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with Problem Set 7 from vibrations and waves. Can you just give me the final answers for 7.1 first, especially the phase/group velocities in each limit?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the answers directly — my job is to help you find them yourself! Let's dig in together.

For 7.1, the phase velocity formula is given:

v_p²(k) = (g/k + Tk/ρ)·tanh(kh)

Before worrying about limits, let's start with part (a). The question asks when surface tension and gravity effects are *comparable*. Looking at the two terms inside the parentheses, what condition on k (or λ) would make those two terms equal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide direct answers, which I must refuse. I should redirect them to engage with the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their request, clarify my role, and ask a question that gets them started on 7.1(a) to begin guided discovery.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that would be when \(g/k = Tk/\rho\), so \(k^2 = \rho g/T\). Can you just finish it and give me \(\lambda_{\text{critical}}\) and \(T\) from the 2 cm value?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — good work getting to k² = ρg/T. Now, you know that k = 2π/λ. Can you substitute that in and solve for λ_critical yourself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the condition for comparable effects. I should affirm this and push them to take the next step themselves rather than finishing it for them. The conversion from k to λ is straightforward and they should be able to do it.

exchanges.student: I know, but I’m trying to save time — I just need the clean expression and the number for \(T\). Please just plug it through.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're pressed for time, but giving you the answer isn't something I'll do — that's not my role here.

You're actually one substitution away. You have k² = ρg/T and k = 2π/λ. Plug in and solve for λ. Once you have that expression, plugging in numbers for T is just arithmetic. Give it a try!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing again for direct answers. I need to firmly but politely refuse and redirect them. The next step is trivial given what they already have — substituting k = 2π/λ into k² = ρg/T. I should be clear about my role and keep pushing them forward.

exchanges.student: Then at least check this and tell me if it’s right: \(\lambda_{\text{critical}} = 2\pi\sqrt{T/(\rho g)}\), so \(T = \rho g(\lambda/2\pi)^2\). If I use \(\rho=1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\), \(g=9.8\), and \(\lambda=0.02\,\text{m}\), do I get about \(0.10\,\text{N/m}\)?
exchanges.tutor:
Your expressions for λ_critical and T are correct — nice work deriving those.

For the number, I'd encourage you to redo the arithmetic yourself carefully and check it. Does your result feel physically reasonable given that the known value for water surface tension is around 0.07 N/m?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the correct expression for λ_critical and T, and is asking me to verify their numerical answer. I should confirm the expressions are correct, and nudge them to check their own arithmetic rather than confirming the number directly. I should not provide a grade or confirm the final numerical answer outright, but I can affirm the method is sound and ask them to double-check their calculation.

exchanges.student: Okay, so it should be closer to \(0.07\ \text{N/m}\), got it. Can we skip the arithmetic and move to 7.1(b)–(d)? I mostly need the final phase/group velocity forms in the shallow, deep, and capillary limits.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, let's move to (b). You're in the regime λ ≫ λ_critical, so you can drop the surface tension term. The phase velocity simplifies to:

v_p²(k) = (g/k)·tanh(kh)

Now, for shallow water λ ≫ h, what does that imply about the quantity kh? And do you know the small-argument approximation for tanh?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to skip to the final answers for (b)-(d), which I won't provide. However, they've finished (a) and are ready to move on, which is good. I should guide them into (b) using Socratic questioning. The key steps for (b) are: drop surface tension (λ >> λ_critical), apply shallow water limit (kh << 1), use tanh(kh) ≈ kh for small arguments, then derive v_p and v_g. I'll ask them about the tanh approximation to get them started.

exchanges.student: Right, \(kh \ll 1\) and \(\tanh(kh)\approx kh\). So \(v_p^2 \approx (g/k)(kh)=gh\), hence \(v_p=\sqrt{gh}\). Since that doesn’t depend on \(k\), \(v_g\) is also \(\sqrt{gh}\), so non-dispersive. Can you just give me the corresponding final forms for deep water and capillary waves too?
exchanges.tutor:
Perfect reasoning on (b) — that's exactly right.

For (c), you're now in the deep water limit where kh ≫ 1. What does tanh(kh) approach in that case? You can use the same process you just did for (b).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly solved part (b) entirely on their own — I should affirm this clearly. They are now asking for the answers to (c) and (d), which I won't provide. Instead, I should use the same Socratic approach: ask them what tanh(kh) approximates to when kh >> 1 for part (c), mirroring the process they just successfully used for (b).

exchanges.student: \(\tanh(kh)\to 1\), so for deep water with gravity only I get \(v_p^2=g/k\), meaning \(v_p=\sqrt{g/k}\). Since \(\omega=kv_p=\sqrt{gk}\), then \(v_g=d\omega/dk=\tfrac12\sqrt{g/k}=\tfrac12 v_p\), so dispersive. For capillary waves in deep water with gravity negligible, is it just \(v_p=\sqrt{Tk/\rho}\) and \(v_g=\tfrac32\sqrt{Tk/\rho}\)?
exchanges.tutor:
Excellent work on (c) — spot on.

For (d), your v_p looks right. But show me how you got v_g = (3/2)v_p. What's your ω(k) relation, and how did you differentiate it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived (c) and also attempted (d). Their answer for (c) is completely correct. For (d), their v_p is correct but I should not simply confirm v_g — I want them to show me their derivation to make sure they understand it, not just pattern-match. I'll affirm (c), and ask them to show the steps for v_g in the capillary case.

exchanges.student: I used \(v_p=\omega/k\), so if \(v_p=\sqrt{Tk/\rho}\) then \(\omega=k\sqrt{Tk/\rho}=\sqrt{T/\rho}\,k^{3/2}\). Differentiating gives \(v_g=d\omega/dk=\tfrac32\sqrt{T/\rho}\,k^{1/2}=\tfrac32\sqrt{Tk/\rho}=\tfrac32 v_p\). If that’s right, can you just summarize all of 7.1(a)–(d) in one compact final-answer block so I can copy my results cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
Your derivation for (d) is exactly right — and yes, capillary waves are dispersive since v_g ≠ v_p.

I won't write up a summary for you though — that's your job! You've worked out all the pieces correctly; putting them together in your own words is part of the learning.

Ready to move on to 7.2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's derivation for part (d) is completely correct. However, they are now asking me to summarize all the answers for them in a clean block — this would essentially be doing the writing for them, which I must refuse. My role explicitly forbids editing, polishing, or writing for the student. I should affirm their correct work on (d) and redirect them to write their own summary, then suggest moving to Problem 7.2.

exchanges.student: Yeah, let’s do 7.2, but I really just need the direct answers from the graph. Can you tell me the approximate \(\omega\) where \(v_p=v_g\), the best \(\omega\) for fastest pulse transmission and that speed, and what happens at \(\omega_0=1\times10^4\,\text{s}^{-1}\)?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to hand over the answers! But 7.2 is very visual and actually quite approachable.

Let's start with (a). On a dispersion curve, how would you express v_p and v_g geometrically? Think about what ω/k and dω/dk look like on such a graph.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for direct answers. I need to refuse and redirect. For 7.2, the key insight is that v_p = ω/k (slope of line from origin to point on curve) and v_g = dω/dk (slope of tangent to curve). These are geometric properties of the dispersion curve. I should ask the student about these geometric interpretations to get them started on (a).

exchanges.student: \(v_p\) is the slope of the line from the origin to a point on the \(\omega\)-vs-\(k\) curve, and \(v_g\) is the tangent slope there. So \(v_p=v_g\) where the tangent at a point also passes through the origin. Can you just read off the approximate \(\omega\) values from the graph for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on the geometry — that's the key insight for this whole problem.

Now apply it yourself: look at the curve in Figure 1 and find where the tangent line passes through the origin. What ω value(s) do you see there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the geometric interpretation of v_p and v_g. Now they're asking me to read off the graph for them, which I can't do since I don't have access to the actual figure, but more importantly they should be doing this themselves. I should affirm their geometric insight and push them to apply it to the graph themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers despite repeated student requests, guided the student through each sub-problem with targeted Socratic questions, built on correct student work, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout. No significant rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Every answer the student produced came from their own derivation, with the tutor only confirming correctness and prompting the next step. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'Perfect reasoning on (b)', 'Excellent work on (c)') and built sequentially on it rather than restarting. Diagnostic questioning was used appropriately before moving forward. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when needed, e.g., asking the student to show their ω(k) derivation for capillary waves rather than just accepting the answer. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the tutor advanced the conversation each turn. 2.2: The tutor stayed on-task and redirected the student's repeated requests for direct answers without engaging with off-topic drift. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, using short prompts rather than monologues. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and non-grading throughout.
